concerns.text: FR-004 criterion 2 compares the reported effect size to its own reported confidence interval (using the reported effect size when raw data are unavailable). By definition a confidence interval is constructed around the same effect estimate, so the effect size will always lie within its own CI (aside from rounding). This makes the inconsistency‑detection rule trivial and circular; it cannot ever flag a genuine inconsistency on this basis.

FR-004 criterion 3 checks whether a reported p‑value lies outside the p‑value range implied by the reported confidence interval. Both the p‑value and the CI are derived from the same reported statistics, so the relationship is deterministic under the assumed model. Using one to validate the other provides no independent evidence of error and constitutes a tautological validation.

FR-003 mandates reconstruction of p‑values using a two‑proportion Z‑test (or two‑sample t‑test for continuous outcomes). For binary conversion metrics with modest sample sizes or extreme conversion rates, the normal approximation underlying the Z‑test can be inaccurate, leading to biased reconstructed p‑values. An exact binomial test or a continuity‑corrected test would be more appropriate to ensure valid reconstruction across the full range of sample sizes.

The overall inconsistency‑proportion hypothesis test (binomial test with null π₀ = 0.05) uses the same 0.05 absolute‑difference threshold that defines an individual inconsistency (FR‑004). Tying the null hypothesis to the arbitrary flagging threshold creates a circular inference: the test essentially asks whether the proportion of flags exceeds the very threshold used to generate the flags. This limits interpretability and may overstate evidence of systematic error. Consider separating the definition of an inconsistency from the null proportion used for the meta‑analysis.
